data.title: FCPT signals $200M term loan deployment through Q3 as it targets 5.4x run-rate leverage
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 marked a continuation of the momentum from 2025 and a strong start to 2026," said (President, CEO & Director William Lenehan), adding that "AFFO per share grew by 3.4% versus the prior year period."
  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said FCPT "acquired $26 million of net lease properties at a 6.8% blended cash cap rate, equivalent to a 7.3% GAAP cap rate," and emphasized that "we're seeing a lot of attractive opportunities and feel good about the strength of our pipeline," while noting "Q2 is shaping out that way so far" on seasonality.
  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) highlighted financing and spread, saying, "We are also excited to have closed on a new $200 million term loan with a 7-year tenor earlier this month" and "The term loan all-in rate is 4.9%, which represents 200 basis points of spread to historical acquisition yields."
  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said tenant-level performance remained strong, citing rent coverage and key brands: "Our rent coverage in Q1 was 5.1x" and "The rent coverage figure for our garden properties specifically is 5.8x," while adding, "we would expect a very high renewal percentage for the spin-off portfolio in the coming years" as lease-extension notices approach.
  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) provided an update on Bahama Breeze exposure and timing, stating, "we own 10 Bahama Breeze properties, which is 1.3% of our ABR," and that Darden plans to convert six locations, while "the remaining 4 properties are 50 basis points of ABR, and we already have actively negotiating letters of intent with new tenants to backfill these locations."
  • "Michael Friedland has joined our Board," said (President, CEO & Director Lenehan), adding that he "recently retired from JPMorgan and brings 30 years of Wall Street experience in real estate finance and corporate credit to FCPT."
  • (CFO & Treasurer Patrick Wernig) described post-quarter-end funding and hedging: "We funded $50 million of the new incremental $200 million term loan in April, and the balance will be used to fund acquisitions in Q2 and Q3," and "we fully hedged our current outstanding term loan balance of $640 million as of April 30 at a blended SOFR rate of 3.1% or approximately 4% all in with that rate steady through November 2027."

Outlook

  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) said, "Following our Q1 results, we are reaffirming our guidance range for 2026 cash G&A of $19.2 million to $19.7 million."
  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) framed expected leverage after deploying the term loan: "Noting that our term loan closed after quarter end, but after fully funding and investing the proceeds, estimated run rate leverage will be 5.4x."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s messaging, management shifted from describing the balance sheet as "over-equitized" (Q4 2025) and discussing broader debt-market attractiveness to a more time-bound funding plan, with (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) saying in Q1 2026, "Our $200 million term loan gives us a direct line of sight for funding between now and Q3."

Financial Results

  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) reported, "AFFO per share was $0.45," and "Cash rental income was $70 million."
  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) said, "Cash G&A expense was $4.9 million for the quarter," and added, "the 70 basis point improvement in operating leverage and flat cash G&A compared to the prior year illustrates our continued efforts at achieving efficient growth and the benefits of our rising scale."
  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) highlighted leasing and rent collections, saying, "We've also continued to make progress with 27 of the 42 leases originally expiring in 2026 extended" and "The recapture rate on these locations is 6% above prior year rent," while also stating, "Our portfolio occupancy remains very strong at 99.6% today" and "We collected 99.7% of base rent in Q1."
  • (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) flagged disclosure changes: "going forward, we plan to disclose GAAP cap rates along with the cash cap rate figure we've always done," and "we are updating the way we disclose AFFO per share growth by calculating without the impact of decimal routing."

Q&A

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: "is that -- as we think about just acquisition activity... should you be able to exceed that and continue to acquire healthily into next year?" (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) responded, "yes... we gave more specific timing guidance than we have in the past," and added, "It's always curious that analysts seem to have declining acquisitions for us... It's not what has been a historical record."
  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: "walk through what you're trying to show" on the new slides; (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said the slide was a response to investor comparisons, adding, "if we were going to trade like a restaurant index, at a minimum, you should weight the index by our rent," and on cap rates, "sometimes we felt our cash cap rates were being compared against their GAAP cap rates."
  • Eric Borden, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: asked about Yum!/Brinker-linked opportunities and development; (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said, "we're always working on those," but noted, "Taco Bell tend to trade for very, very tight cap rates."
  • Eric Borden, BMO Capital Markets Equity Research: asked about bad debt; (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) answered, "the number is 0 for the year-to-date," and added, "we have not had any bad debt this year."
  • Wesley Golladay, Robert W. Baird & Co.: asked about renewals and the 6% recapture; (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) responded, "I wouldn't overemphasize it" and added, "Our typical rent growth is 1.5%. If you're modeling our company, I think that's a good place to go."
  • William John Kilichowski, Wells Fargo Securities: asked about Bahama Breeze downtime and rent loss; (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said, "No, I don't think there will be downtime" and "We feel really good about being able to re-lease these to strong tenants," with (CFO & Treasurer Wernig) adding, "it's... 4 stores and 50 basis points of ABR."
  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities: asked about new sectors and competitive landscape; (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) said, "Yes, I think that's a good way of thinking about it" on TAM expansion, and on competition added, "I don't think we have evidence" yet of private credit pullback, while also saying, "We can build a portfolio throughout a year... but we have the scale to do bigger things as well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated pressure on acquisition cadence, cap rate messaging, and credit items (bad debt; Bahama Breeze), including Goldsmith’s focus on whether the term loan implied “shadow guidance” and Kilichowski probing downtime and rent loss.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, with (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) emphasizing offense and funding visibility: "our existing portfolio strength is compelling for us to focus on offense" and "Our $200 million term loan gives us a direct line of sight for funding between now and Q3," while giving brief, direct answers on items like yields ("Small sample size").
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone remained confident on portfolio quality and credit, but shifted from broader capital-market positioning to execution detail around the new term loan and incremental disclosure changes.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 introduced a completed financing event and deployment timeline ("funded $50 million... in April" and using the balance in "Q2 and Q3"), while Q4 2025 emphasized balance sheet positioning ("over-equitized") and multiple avenues for future debt.
  • Q1 2026 tightened the Bahama Breeze narrative from early-stage discussions (Q4) to active LOIs and a split between conversions and backfills, alongside explicit statements about continued rent collection windows: "we'll continue to collect rent throughout the backfill process."
  • Q1 2026 added governance and disclosure updates (board appointment; GAAP cap rate and AFFO rounding disclosures), which were not featured as headline items in Q4 2025.
  • Analyst focus shifted from leverage ceiling and assorted credit topics (Q4) toward acquisition visibility tied to the $200 million term loan and the purpose of new investor-deck slides (Q1).

Risks and Concerns

  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) identified seller pricing as a constraint, stating, "the limiting factor on new sectors and deals is typically sellers lofty pricing expectations."
  • On Bahama Breeze, management characterized remaining re-tenanting as manageable but dependent on negotiations, with (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) saying, "the timing and final economics will ultimately depend on the outcome of these negotiations," while also stating FCPT expects to "recover or possibly even exceed the prior rent."
  • (President, CEO & Director Lenehan) flagged competitive conditions as uncertain, noting discussion around private credit but saying, "I don't think we have evidence of that yet" regarding pullback.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a continuation of momentum, highlighted a $200 million, 7-year term loan with a 4.9% all-in rate to fund acquisitions through Q3, and reiterated confidence in tenant quality and rent coverage. Executives emphasized minimal bad debt and high occupancy, provided a more developed plan for Bahama Breeze conversions and backfills while continuing rent collection, and reaffirmed 2026 cash G&A guidance of $19.2 million to $19.7 million. Management also pointed to new disclosure practices around GAAP cap rates and AFFO growth calculation, and announced the addition of Michael Friedland to the board.

data.title: Western Digital anticipates Q4 revenue $3.65B ± $100M with EPS $3.25 ± $0.15 as gross margin is guided to 51%–52%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Western Digital (WDC) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • “WD started calendar year 2026 with great execution, driving strong sequential and year-over-year revenue growth in our cloud, consumer and client businesses while expanding gross and operating margins,” said CEO Tiang Yew Tan, adding that “gross margin exceeded 50%” and that WD is “a focused HDD company and a strategic partner to hyperscalers and cloud service providers in this AI-driven data economy.”

  • Tan tied demand to AI data creation and reiterated a long-range demand view: “we expect agentic AI to drive a step function increase in capacity-oriented storage demand,” and “this reinforces our conviction that long-term data storage growth will be greater than 25% CAGR.”

  • On the product roadmap and customer qualification status, Tan said, “Our high-capacity drive road map now extends from our 44-terabyte HAMR and 40-terabyte ePMR drives that are currently in qualification to a road map that goes beyond 100-terabytes,” and added, “we are now in qualification with 4 customers” for HAMR and “qualifying our 40-terabyte ePMR drives with 3 customers and are on track to start volume production in the second half of calendar year 2026.”

  • CFO Kris Sennesael framed the quarter as execution plus customer alignment: “The WD team delivered strong results,” and reported “revenue was $3.3 billion,” “earnings per share was $2.72,” and “we delivered 222 exabytes,” while noting cloud concentration: “Cloud represented 89% of total revenue at $3 billion.”

Outlook

  • “We anticipate revenue to be $3.65 billion, plus/minus $100 million,” Sennesael said, with “gross margin ... 51% to 52%,” “operating expenses ... $385 million to $395 million,” “interest and other expenses ... $10 million,” “tax rate ... 16%,” and “diluted earnings per share ... $3.25 plus/minus $0.15.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guidance cadence and language, management emphasized increased multi-year visibility; Tan said, “Our long-term visibility continues to improve with the duration of our agreements now extending into calendar year '28 and calendar year '29.”

Financial results

  • Sennesael reported segment revenue details: “Consumer represented 6% of revenue at $186 million,” and “Client represented 5% of total revenue at $179 million,” alongside consolidated profitability metrics including “gross margin ... 50.5%,” “operating income ... $1.3 billion,” and “operating margin of 38.6%.”

  • On cash generation and capital returns, Sennesael said, “Operating cash flow ... was $1.1 billion,” “free cash flow generation of $978 million,” and “we made $43 million of dividend payments and increased our share repurchases to $752 million,” adding, “Since the launch of our capital return program ... we have returned $2.2 billion to our shareholders.”

  • On balance sheet actions, Sennesael said WD “monetiz[ed] 5.8 million shares of SanDisk, which led to a $3.1 billion reduction in our debt,” leaving “only $1.6 billion of convertible debt,” and concluding, “we ended the quarter in a net positive cash position of $450 million.”

  • The Board action highlighted a dividend step-up: “the Board has approved a 20% increase of the cash dividend ... to $0.15 per share ... payable on June 17, 2026,” Sennesael said.

Q&A

  • Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley: asked how “agentic AI” maps to HDD workflows and the “greater than 25%” exabyte view; CEO Tan: “We really see 3 core drivers ... training ... agentic AI and inferencing ... physical AI ... That’s why we have the confidence to see exabyte growth growing beyond 25% CAGR.”

  • Woodring, Morgan Stanley: questioned Q4 gross margin guide implying slower expansion; CFO Sennesael: “For Q4, we are guiding to 51% to 52%,” and pointed to “pricing ... mix ... and ... driv[ing] adoption of UltraSMR” as margin levers.

  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: asked what enabled the pricing step-up and whether it reflects LTAs; CEO Tan: “pricing was up 9% year-on-year,” driven by “timing of new LTAs coming on board” and “better TCO value.”

  • Daryanani, Evercore ISI: asked about cost per exabyte declines; CEO Tan: “we delivered 10% year-on-year,” and added an UltraSMR target: “by the end of fiscal year '27, close to about 60% of all the exabytes that we ship will be on UltraSMR.”

  • Aaron Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about capacity investment needs to meet structural demand; CEO Tan: “we still do not see any need to increase unit capacity ... Our focus is really to continue to improve aerial density.”

  • Rakers, Wells Fargo: asked about capital return versus building cash; CFO Sennesael: “We are returning all the excess free cash flow back to the shareholders,” via “a combination of our dividend program and share buyback program.”

  • Thomas O’Malley, Barclays: pressed on customer appetite for continued pricing increases and LTA structures; CEO Tan: “our pricing philosophy is really to provide predictable pricing,” and said LTAs “extend into calendar year '29,” with “a degree of pricing associated with it.”

  • Michael Cadiz, Citigroup: asked on yields/reliability; CEO Tan: ePMR yields “continue to be in the 90% range,” and “we don’t see any changes.”

  • Cadiz, Citigroup: asked whether HDD demand is tied to flash/HDD price differentials and if architectures are changing; CEO Tan: “we don’t see any ... major structural changes to architecture,” and described “a very symbiotic relationship” between HDD and flash in inference stacks.

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked why gross margin outperformed revenue outperformance; CFO Sennesael: “pricing ... was a little bit better ... mix ... and ... driv[ing] down costs across the board.”

  • Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked why cost declines might not accelerate with 40TB ePMR and HAMR; CFO Sennesael: “There is going to be an adoption curve ... the improvements will be phased in over the ramp period.”

  • Aisling Grueninger, BofA: asked where UltraSMR JBOD expands reach and materiality; CEO Tan: “expand our reach into Tier 2 CSPs,” and reiterated that “close to 60% of the exabytes that we ship will be on UltraSMR” by end of fiscal 2027.

  • Christopher Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked what is embedded in multi-year agreement pricing; CEO Tan: “There may be periods of pricing adjustment as we introduce new capacity points.”

  • Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald: asked about the remaining SanDisk stake timing/taxes; CFO Sennesael: “It’s our intention to monetize the remaining 1.7 million shares ... before the end of calendar year '26 ... in a tax-free manner.”

  • Karl Ackerman, BNP Paribas: asked about head/media capacity adds and prepayments; CEO Tan: “we’re ... not making any investments in terms of adding unit capacity,” but “if it makes economic sense, we’ll look to add head and media capacity.”

  • Ackerman, BNP Paribas: asked how far out commitments are POs vs LTAs; CFO Sennesael: “most of the purchase orders are being placed a year in advance,” and “beyond the first year, we are going into those ... frameworks.”

  • Hadi Orabi, TD Cowen: asked whether hyperscaler demand patterns diverge; CEO Tan: “in general, the profile is quite similar.”

  • James Schneider, Goldman Sachs: asked expected HAMR share of exabytes by end of 2027; CEO Tan: “We don’t have a number ... right now,” emphasizing a focus to “derisk the transition.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on durability and limits (pricing endurance, gross-margin sustainability, capacity investment needs), including questions such as how much “appetite do customers have to keep on taking pricing increases?” (Thomas O’Malley, Barclays) and whether there are “emerging headwinds” in margins (Erik Woodring, Morgan Stanley).

  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and remained steady in Q&A, frequently using confidence language tied to execution and visibility, including “we remain confident in our ability to deliver sustainable revenue growth” (CFO Kris Sennesael) and “we have the confidence to see exabyte growth growing beyond 25% CAGR” (CEO Tiang Yew Tan).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management used more explicit “visibility” and longer-duration agreement language (now citing extensions “into calendar year '28 and calendar year '29”), while analyst focus stayed concentrated on pricing power, margins, and the pace of technology transitions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Strategic narrative broadened from Q2’s emphasis on being “pretty much sold out for calendar year '26” and initial qualification starts, to Q3 detailing deeper qualification scale (“in qualification with 4 customers” for HAMR) and a longer roadmap “beyond 100-terabytes.”

  • Capital allocation messaging shifted from Q2’s debt-reduction intent using SanDisk monetization to Q3’s reported completion of a large debt reduction and an explicit dividend increase; Sennesael said the Q3 SanDisk monetization “led to a $3.1 billion reduction in our debt,” and the Board approved a dividend increase to “$0.15 per share.”

  • Analyst questioning in both quarters repeatedly returned to gross-margin durability and cost-down pace, while Q3 added more direct probing of pricing step-ups and the architecture relationship between HDD and flash.

Risks and concerns

  • Management repeatedly framed execution risk around qualification and ramp timing, including yield/reliability gating for HAMR; Sennesael said there is “still a little bit of work to be done in terms of yield and reliability and quality” for HAMR even as “good progress” continues.

  • Demand fulfillment risk was addressed primarily through mix and aerial density rather than unit capacity adds; Tan said, “we still do not see any need to increase unit capacity,” and positioned “improv[ing] aerial density” and mix-up to higher capacity drives as the main mitigations.

Final takeaway

Western Digital management described Q3 as a period of strong demand and pricing with expanding profitability, and paired that with a Q4 outlook calling for higher revenue, continued gross margin expansion, and higher EPS. Executives emphasized AI-driven data creation—especially inference and agentic AI—as a durable driver of HDD capacity demand, highlighted expanding UltraSMR adoption targets, and described multi-year customer agreements extending into 2028–2029. Management also pointed to a strengthened balance sheet after SanDisk monetization, an increased dividend, and an ongoing framework to return “all the excess free cash flow” through dividends and buybacks.
